Day Trip · 30 Minutes North of Watch Hill

A Day at Foxwoods Casino from Watch Hill

Thirty minutes north of Watch Hill sits the largest resort casino in North America — six casinos, three hotels, a tribal history museum that's quietly excellent, and enough non-gambling things to do that the trip works even if you don't gamble at all.

Updated May 26, 2026 · 10 min read · Written from Westerly, RI

The Short Answer

Worth the drive even if you don't gamble. The Mashantucket Pequot Museum is a genuinely substantive attraction most visitors don't know exists. The concerts at Grand Theater bring real touring acts. Parking is free, the casino floor is free to enter, and Tanger Outlets is on the same property. Allow a full day if you want to do more than gamble.

Quick Facts

Distance from Watch Hill
30 min / 25 miles
Size
Largest casino resort in N. America
Casinos under one roof
Six
Table games
250+ tables, 22 game types
Legal gambling age
21 (Bingo: 18)
Parking
Free, multiple decks

Foxwoods is one of the more genuinely unusual destinations in New England — a 9-million-square-foot resort complex on the Mashantucket Pequot Reservation in eastern Connecticut, owned and operated entirely by the Tribe. Six casinos. Three hotels. A bingo hall that seats 3,600. Over 250 table games. More than 35 restaurants. A 70-store outlet mall. A 33-story zipline. A Native American history museum that opened in 1998 and is one of the largest of its kind in the country. Over 40,000 visitors a day.

And it's a 30-minute drive from Watch Hill — through Mystic, then 15 minutes inland on Route 2 — which makes it the easiest unusual day trip available to anyone staying in the village. Even if gambling isn't your thing (and it isn't most travelers'), Foxwoods rewards a half-day visit. The complex is a destination unto itself, and the Mashantucket Pequot Museum is the kind of place that surprises people who walked in expecting only slot machines.

What follows is the locally honest version — what's worth your time at Foxwoods, what isn't, how to do it efficiently, and the one attraction most travel guides under-emphasize.

The History

The Mashantucket Pequot Tribal Nation is one of the oldest Indigenous nations in North America, with a continuous presence in southeastern Connecticut dating back at least 10,000 years. The Pequot War of 1636–1638 devastated the Tribe — the Mystic Massacre of 1637 killed an estimated 400–700 Pequot people, and the surviving population was scattered, enslaved, or restricted to small reservations.

The Mashantucket Pequot Reservation was formally re-established in the late 20th century after decades of advocacy. In 1986 the Tribe opened a high-stakes bingo hall on the reservation — the legal mechanism that allowed eventual casino gaming. Table games were added in 1992, slots in 1993, and the property has been expanding ever since.

Today Foxwoods is the economic engine of the Tribe — funding tribal government, education, healthcare, and the museum. The Mashantucket Pequot Museum and Research Center, which opened in 1998, is the Tribe's own narrative of its history, told in its own voice. That detail matters: this isn't a third-party history of the Pequot people. It's the Tribe presenting its own story, in a building the Tribe built and runs.

What to Know Before You Go

Don't miss the Mashantucket Pequot Museum

This is the under-the-radar attraction most visitors skip. The Museum and Research Center sits on the same complex as the casino and is one of the largest Native American museums in the country. Permanent exhibits cover 18,000 years of regional Indigenous history, the Pequot War, and the Tribe's modern revitalization. There's also a 185-foot observation tower with panoramic views. Allow 2-3 hours. Admission is reasonable (around $20 adult, less for kids and seniors) and entirely separate from anything happening at the casino.

Getting there

From Watch Hill: 30 minutes via I-95 W to exit 92, then Route 2 W. Parking at Foxwoods is free across multiple covered decks — the Grand Pequot Tower deck and the Fox Tower deck are the most central. You do not need to stay overnight to park; day visitors are explicitly welcome. Avoid Friday evening arrivals if possible; concert and gaming traffic on Route 2 can back up.

The casino floor — what to know if you're new

Gambling age is 21 for all table games, slots, and the race book. Bingo is 18. The casino floor is free to walk through, free to watch, and free to enter — you only spend money if you gamble or buy food. There's no dress code on the gaming floor. ATMs are everywhere but the fees are high; bring cash or use the bank-style cashier cages where withdrawals are cheaper. New gamblers: blackjack tables typically have the lowest house edge among the main games.

Best time to visit

Weekday afternoons are the most relaxed — lighter crowds at the casino, easier reservations at the better restaurants, no concert traffic. Weekend evenings are the most energetic — full casino floor, concerts at Grand Theater, packed restaurants. Sunday brunch at Cedars Steaks & Oysters is a quiet local secret. The Mashantucket Pequot Museum is least crowded on weekday mornings.

Where to eat

Foxwoods has 35+ restaurants ranging from food court to fine dining. The serious dinner options: Cedars Steaks & Oysters for the upscale steakhouse experience, Al Dente for southern Italian, Paragon at the top of the Fox Tower for high-end with views. Hard Rock Cafe is the reliable mid-tier choice. The food court is more interesting than most casino food courts — there are several genuinely good quick-service options. Avoid Sugar Factory unless you're committed to that specific bachelorette aesthetic.

Non-gambling things to do

If you don't gamble at all, you can still spend a full day here: the Pequot Museum (3 hours), Tanger Outlets shopping (1-2 hours, 70+ stores), Foxwoods HighFlyer zipline (the largest in Connecticut, launched from the 33-story Fox Tower), the Thrill Tower (Sky Drop and Sky Launch), Monza karting, TopGolf Swing Suite, High Roller Luxury Lanes bowling, and the G Spa. The casino's Grand Theater hosts major touring acts; check the schedule before your visit.

Mohegan Sun vs Foxwoods

The two casinos are 15 minutes apart and very different. Foxwoods is larger, more spread out, more family-friendly, and has the museum. Mohegan Sun is more concentrated, has a flashier casino floor, and is generally considered to have a better restaurant lineup. If you can only visit one, Foxwoods is the better choice for a day trip; Mohegan Sun is better as an overnight party destination. If you have time, do both — they're an easy combined day.

Most Watch Hill visitors do Foxwoods as a day trip and return to the village in the evening. If you want to stay overnight at Foxwoods itself, the on-property hotels are mid-range. But Watch Hill remains the better base for almost everything else in the area.

Browse Watch Hill stays →

Frequently Asked Questions

How far is Foxwoods from Watch Hill, Rhode Island?

About 25 miles or 30 minutes by car. The route runs through Mystic, then up Route 2 W into Mashantucket. There's no public transit from Watch Hill — you'll need a car. Many Watch Hill hotels can arrange car service if requested.

Is Foxwoods worth visiting if I don't gamble?

Yes. The Mashantucket Pequot Museum and Research Center is one of the most substantive Native American museums in the country and is the standout reason to visit even for non-gamblers. The complex also has Tanger Outlets shopping, the HighFlyer zipline, Thrill Tower attractions, multiple restaurants, and the Grand Theater for concerts. A full day at Foxwoods without gambling is genuinely possible.

What is the Mashantucket Pequot Museum?

It's the Tribe's own history museum — one of the largest Native American museums in the country, opened in 1998 on the reservation. Permanent exhibits cover 18,000 years of Indigenous history in the region, including the Pequot War, the near-destruction and modern revitalization of the Tribe, and a 185-foot observation tower with panoramic views. Allow 2-3 hours. Admission is around $20 for adults.

How many casinos are at Foxwoods?

Six casinos under one roof, plus a separate race book and one of the world's largest bingo halls (3,600 seats). The complex has more than 250 table games covering 22 game types, including 100 poker tables. Foxwoods is the largest casino resort in North America.

What's the gambling age at Foxwoods?

21 for all table games, slots, the race book, and Keno. The legal age for Bingo is 18. ID is checked at the door if you look anywhere close to the line.

Is parking free at Foxwoods?

Yes — parking is free across multiple covered parking decks. Day visitors are explicitly welcome; you do not need to be a hotel guest to park or to use the casinos, restaurants, or attractions. The most central decks are at the Grand Pequot Tower and Fox Tower.

Should I visit Foxwoods or Mohegan Sun?

Foxwoods is larger, more spread out, has the museum, and is more family-friendly. Mohegan Sun is more concentrated, has a generally better restaurant lineup, and a flashier atmosphere. They're 15 minutes apart on Route 2 — many visitors who have time do both on the same trip. For a single day trip from Watch Hill with non-gambling activity, Foxwoods is the better choice.

Is Foxwoods open year-round?

Yes — Foxwoods operates 24/7, 365 days a year. The casinos never close. Some specific restaurants, attractions, and concert schedules vary; check the calendar before specific bookings.
